content: Thanks for taking the time. I am trying to understand how this situation works in your real workflow. I am not here to sell anything. I will mostly ask about what you already do today and recent examples. Ready to get started?
Tell me about the last customer interview you conducted for discovery — not a sales call, but one where you were trying to learn whether to build something.
Last week actually. We're exploring a feature that would let users export their dashboard data to Excel automatically. I talked to three customers who'd requested it in the past.
Walk me through what you did after one of those calls ended. What happened next?

I had some rough notes in a Google Doc during the call — mostly bullet points. After the call I spent maybe 30 minutes writing up a summary of what they said, their main pain points, and whether I thought we should build it. Then I shared the doc with my co-founder.
You mentioned rough notes during the call. What usually gets missed or lost when you're taking notes live?

Honestly, the specific examples. Like someone will say 'last month we had an issue with this' and give me a detailed story, but I'm trying to listen and type at the same time so I just write 'export problem' and move on. Later I can't remember the details.
Has that ever caused a real problem — where missing those details led to a wrong decision or wasted time?

Yeah, actually. Six months ago we built a Slack integration because three customers said they 'would love that.' Turns out when we shipped it, none of them used it. When I went back to my notes, I realized they'd all said 'would' — future tense — but I treated it like validated demand. If I'd captured the actual words they used, I would've seen they were just being polite.
That must have been frustrating. What did that cost you — in terms of time or money or opportunity cost?
Two engineers worked on it for about three weeks, so roughly $15k in loaded cost. Plus the opportunity cost of not building something users actually needed. It was a painful lesson.
